title: Deep litter coop management
subtitle: How to build and maintain a deep litter bed in a chicken coop over autumn and winter
excerpt: A deep litter bed turns coop waste into a warm, composting floor that reduces ammonia, cuts cleaning time, and gives hens a scratching surface all winter. This guide covers how to start the bed in autumn and keep it healthy through to the spring clear-out.
sourceNotes: Harvey Ussery, The Small-Scale Poultry Flock (2011), chapter on deep litter. Jeff Mattocks and the Fertrell Company, Poultry Nutrition Notes, deep litter fermentation overview. UK Poultry Club welfare guidelines on litter management.

glossaryTerms.definition: A managed coop floor system in which carbon-rich bedding is layered and turned regularly so that microbial activity composts the waste in place. A healthy bed stays warm and low in ammonia through fermentation.

The balance between carbon-rich material (straw, wood shavings) and nitrogen-rich material (manure) in a composting system. A ratio of around 25:1 to 30:1 keeps decomposition active without excess ammonia or odour.
A gut disease in chickens caused by Eimeria protozoa. Wet, compacted litter that is not properly managed raises the protozoan load. Symptoms include bloody droppings, lethargy, and hunched posture.

content.text: The
deep litter

method lets you clean the coop less often and give hens something to do in winter. Done right, the bed heats gently from below, smells earthy rather than sharp, and produces finished compost in spring.
How it works
Microbes break down manure and bedding in place. The process only works when the
carbon-to-nitrogen ratio
stays balanced. Too little carbon and ammonia builds up. Too much moisture and the bed goes anaerobic and sour.
Setting up the bed in autumn

Start with a clean, dry coop floor. Lay a base layer of wood shavings or chopped straw at least 15 cm deep. A shallower bed compacts too quickly and loses airflow. Spread the first layer flat and tamp it lightly. The hens will start scratching it within hours.
Remove all old litter and scrub the floor before starting. Pathogens from a previous flock persist in old material.
Lay 15 cm of wood shavings or chopped straw. Avoid fresh sawdust from treated timber.
Add a light sprinkling of dry garden soil or a poultry litter conditioner. This seeds the bed with useful microbes.
Let the hens in. They will mix and scratch from the start.
Maintaining the bed through winter

Check the bed every week. Push a hand 10 cm into the litter. It should feel warm but not hot. It should smell earthy, not of ammonia. If you smell ammonia, the carbon balance is off. Add a fresh layer of shavings and fork the surface to reintroduce air.

Add 5 cm of fresh material each month or whenever the surface looks flat and compacted. The hens do most of the turning. In a small coop with many birds you may need to fork the litter yourself once a week to keep it loose.

attrs.title: Watch for wet patches under drinkers
attrs.body: Spilled water is the main cause of a failed deep litter bed. Wet litter compacts, goes anaerobic, and raises the risk of coccidiosis in young birds. Mount drinkers on a raised platform or use nipple drinkers on the wall. Check the area under the drinker at every top-up visit and remove wet clumps before adding fresh material.

content.text: Managing the risk of coccidiosis
A well-managed dry bed does not raise the risk of
coccidiosis

above that of a conventionally managed coop. The risk rises when litter becomes wet and compacted. Adult hens with prior exposure carry immunity. Young birds on deep litter for the first time are the most vulnerable. Keep chicks off the bed until they are fully feathered at around eight weeks old.
The spring clear-out

Clear the bed once in spring, not mid-winter. Moving hens out in cold weather causes stress and the composted material is at its best in March or April. Remove everything down to bare floor, scrub and dry the coop, then apply a poultry-safe disinfectant before starting a fresh bed. The cleared litter goes straight onto the vegetable garden as a nitrogen-rich mulch.

entries.cue: Strong ammonia smell at nose height inside the coop
entries.cause: Too much nitrogen, not enough carbon, or the bed has become anaerobic from moisture
entries.fix: Fork the whole bed to the bottom. Remove visibly wet areas. Add a 10 cm layer of dry wood shavings on top. If the smell persists after 48 hours, remove half the litter and replace it.

entries.cue: Litter looks compacted and flat within two weeks
entries.cause: Too many birds for the floor area, or bedding type is too fine and mats quickly
entries.fix: Switch to coarser material such as chopped straw or hemp bedding. Fork every three to four days in the first month while the bed gets established.

entries.cue: Bed feels cold throughout even after adding fresh material
entries.cause: Microbial activity has not established, usually because the bed dried out completely or started too shallow
entries.fix: Fork in a small amount of finished garden compost to re-seed the microbial population. Add moisture by misting with plain water, enough to feel damp when squeezed but not dripping.
